3	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 6	Fascinations Overview
Fascinations
OVERVIEW
(00:01)
Yo. So I want to go over fascinations with you in this module here. Fascinations
are one of my favorite things on earth. They’re one of the things that I think
really helped me to improve as a copywriter dramatically. So what are
fascinations? They’re also sometimes referred to as curiosity bullets and
according to Tony Flores and Clayton Makepeace, the point of a fascination
is to stimulate curiosity and intrigue, get the prospect to keep reading, build
desire and create an urge to know the secret that the bullet reveals, capture
and keep your prospects attention, make your message more readable, add
more dimensions to your promotion and crush headline block. We’re going
to look at tons of examples so don’t worry about that but this is a pretty
good breakthrough from these guys or a breakdown from these guys and it’s
something where you’ll see in action in a minute.
(01:04)
So where do you often see fascinations? Headlines, lead, product description
especially for books and courses as well as ads and there’s several categories
of fascinations that I personally use a lot.
(01:16)
Oh, what I was going to say earlier, honestly, there’s a document from the
Screaming Eagle newsletter that AWAI, I guess teaches or has the rights to or
whatever that has like 23, I believe, types of fascinations in them and it was
written by Tony with Clay and it was part of his newsletter originally. I have a
copy of it, but I don’t know how I got it originally. Honestly, you can Google it
and it’s just available online, but I don’t want to teach off of that or use their
intellectual property or anything when I’m recording this video because I’m
obviously going to be selling this course. But beyond that, too, honestly, it’s
an amazing document that really, you should look it up or buy it or pay for it
or whatever it takes to get it. I don’t know if you have to pay AWAI or what, but
it’s really worth it. It’s a really great document.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

With that being said, this is sort of like the main bullets that I use and I’ve
named them. Some of them just are very basic, but some of these are going
to be my own names for them or whatever. This is without looking at their

is what I personally use, the ones that I use most commonly, and I’m going to
go through them with you and then I just wrote examples from scratch over
the last I don’t know, half an hour just making a bunch of stuff up, which you’ll
see, but it’ll give you a really clear picture of example.
(02:44)
There’s like why, how, when, what, the secret bullet, the list bullet, the never
bullet, the contrarian bullet, the named oddity bullet, the speedy bullet and the
plus bullet. These are the main ones that I use, but let me show you what it
actually looks like in action.
(03:01)
So here’s like why. Why people with high blood pressure should never go for
a swim in a coordinated pool. Why this Nobel Prize-winning scientist from
Louisiana believes he can predict hurricanes three months in advance. Why 4
out of 5 car salesman surveyed refused to drive American made automobiles.
Hint: It has nothing to do with engineering. Why global warming will mean
hotter profits for these 7 tech stocks. Again, I just totally made these up, all
of them. I mean there is no Nobel Prize-winning scientists who thinks he can
predict hurricanes or 4 out of 5 car salesman.
(03:38)
But the point is if you had this, you wouldn’t make this up in your promotion.
That’s truly not the point. The point is if you’re going through a book or a guide
or a course; there’s a bunch of interesting information in there and you’re
taking it and turning it into - you’re creating bullets based off of that and I can
actually probably show. That’s a good idea. I’m going to do that after we get
through the training.
(04:00)
But the point is if you see this stuff, so if it was in the lead, which you now
know about, the lead of the copy which has fascinations and say it’s a blood
pressure offer and you’re talking about how there you’re going to show them
a way to have healthy blood pressure and you’re like, “You’re going to find

out more in just a few minutes. Plus in a few seconds from now, I’m going to

(04:34)
So even if your lead didn’t hit entirely, if you didn’t have the best lead, they see a
riddle because fascinations are really like riddles or puzzles and they see that
and then they’re like, “Oh man, I’ve got to keep reading so that I can find out
the answer to this riddle.” Or if you put it in the products, if you’re selling the
products, an info product like a book or a video guide or a chorus or whatever,
or even a live and this works great for webinars and live events. You’re like,
“When you attend this free training about disaster preparedness, you’re going
to get blah, blah, blah. Plus, you’ll also discover why this Nobel-Prize winning
scientist from Louisiana believes he can predict hurricanes three months in

these 7 tech stocks.” I mean, maybe they’re not related, but “you’ll get the

-- 7 of 12 --

8	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 6	Fascinations Overview
(12:32)
The never bullet. Why you should never grill meat in the weeks following a
natural disaster. The 5 words you should never say to a police officer during
a crisis. The handy little hack that can assure you never get another speeding
ticket as long as you live even if you act like a NASCAR driver behind the
wheel. So the never stuff is what never to do and you could also obviously,
use it like a never again type thing as well.
(12:56)
The contrarian bullet. These are exactly what they sound like just being
contrarian bullets. This is how I define them. This is again, my own style or
version of this. So brushing your teeth is the best way to avoid gum disease,
right? Nope. See why dentists now say swishing this silver-colored liquid in
your mouth is 10x more effective. Again, if we were selling a book of natural
remedies, you can be like page 45. Think turmeric will stop inflammation?
Wrong. Here’s three kitchen spices that you should use instead. Contrarian;
it’s the right wrong type thing. The truth about salt and why more may be better
when it comes to your cholesterol. So again, just a very simple contrarian
thing if you had a product or a part of your mechanism and a health offer or
whatever it was about how more salt actually can be good for you.
(13:49)
The named oddity bullets; this is where you’re just taking something and you’re
giving it a cool name. So this is where you can make something up if you
have a interesting thing. Here’s some example, it’s like, why using something
called the “Peking Duck Grip” can make you up to 3x more deadly with a knife.
Totally made that up, totally made up but I love it. So if the product talks about
how, I don’t know, a Chinese knife technique and you can find a way to relate
it, you can totally use the peking duck grip. How something called “Weeping
Willow Syndrome” could explain the real reason you feel less balanced during
menopause. Fuck, I love fascinations. It’s so fun! I think these oddity ones are
my favorite, too by the way.
(14:30)
The “Black Chalk Equation” famed economist John Maynard Keynes used
to predict every single recession in his lifetime, usually up to six months in
advance. Again, I don’t think John Mayer Keynes actually predicted every
recession in his lifetime, but naming it like that, saying he did even and he
would write out his equations on a chalkboard but now I’m just am calling it
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

The speedy bullet. So speed bullet is just time frames, getting things done

“Do you know why you should never put a huge fence around your house? Do
you know why you should never eat grilled meat in a crisis? Do you know why

away immediately once the nationwide disaster strikes? It’s okay if you don’t,

You’ll get the right answers in just a moment - answers that you won’t find in
any preparedness book” and it goes on from here.
(17:27)
This is the whole offer but what I want to show you now is that wasn’t the lead,
but now I’m going to scroll, I’m scrolling. Now we’re down to the products and
again, you’ll know i had thicker paragraphs back then, stuff like that. Now I
write a little bit more sparse.
(17:49)
“Inside the Bulletproof Home, you’ll discover” and I probably went over the
top with these fascinations because I was so obsessed. This is probably too

(18:03)
“How to break apart a mob by saying one simple word. It can be a crowd of
100 or more, but just uttering this word will have them turning around and
quickly running away. There’s no violence and no injuries from combat and
your family stays safe from a horde of ransackers and vigilantes. The 4 basic
principles to separate a mob from a military unit. With this knowledge, you will
quickly be able to identify their level of threat and take fast, decisive action
to neutralize them. The fastest way possible to demoralize a mob and have
them running for the hills. It’s based on exploiting their one glaring weakness
and it can be the difference between peace and bloodshed.
(18:33)
Why there’s a huge difference between “cover” and “concealment” and why one

So I kind of went a little overboard, but the point is, I just want to show you

like, Worried about survival during a crisis? Here’s why you should never put a
huge fence around your house. Something like that. Why you should never eat
grilled meat in a crisis. I mean, you could just totally use these as headlines
